title: Bounded Contexts & Data Ownership
summary: A bounded context is the boundary within which a microservice has exclusive ownership of its data and logic. No other service may access its database directly — all communication goes through the service's API. This is the foundational rule of microservice design and comes from Domain-Driven Design (DDD).
keyPoints: Each service owns its data exclusively — no shared databases
Other services must query data through the owning service's API
Bounded contexts map to real business domains (users, games, activity)
Polyglot persistence: each service can use a different database type
Polyglot programming: each service can use a different language or framework
Conway's Law: system architecture tends to mirror the team communication structure
Splitting services along domain boundaries reduces inter-team coupling

exercises.prompt: In a microservices architecture, which rule defines a bounded context?
options: A service owns its data exclusively — no other service can touch its database directly
A service shares its database with at most one other service
A service is limited to a single HTTP endpoint
A service must be deployed on its own server
exercises.answer: A service owns its data exclusively — no other service can touch its database directly
exercises.explanation: Shared databases create tight coupling — a schema change in one service can silently break another. Exclusive ownership enforces clear contracts.

exercises.prompt: Conway's Law states that a system's architecture tends to mirror what?
options: The communication structure of the organisation that built it
The programming language chosen for each service
The number of developers on the team
The deployment frequency of the system
exercises.answer: The communication structure of the organisation that built it
exercises.explanation: Teams tend to build systems that reflect how they communicate. Acknowledging this helps you design team structures that produce the architecture you want.

exercises.prompt: Why does a notification-service written in Node.js fit correctly in a Python microservices architecture?
options: Microservices allow each service to use the best technology for its job — language choice is a per-service decision
Python cannot send emails or push notifications
Node.js services are always faster than Python services
The course required at least one non-Python service
exercises.answer: Microservices allow each service to use the best technology for its job — language choice is a per-service decision
exercises.explanation: Each service is independently deployed and communicates over HTTP or a message queue, so internal implementation details — including language — don't affect other services.
